findings.rationale: Both terms refer to the sulfur content measured in a material test certificate, with identical domain and range.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term has no definition, so while the matching name and property type suggest the same concept, the absence of a stated meaning prevents asserting interchangeable EXACT equivalence and our cast-analysis-specific framing may not align precisely.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same name, domain (MaterialTestCertificate), and describe the finishing/delivery condition recorded on a certificate.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream property shares our highly specific name and property type (suggesting the same MTC finishing concept), but with no upstream definition to confirm identical domain, scope, and value semantics, EXACT cannot be justified and the stricter CLOSE is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the ISO‑3166 country where steel is melted and poured, with identical domain (IronSteelProduct) and range.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term shares an identical, highly domain-specific name strongly implying the same melt-and-pour origin concept, but with no upstream definition, domain, or range to confirm interchangeability I downgrade from EXACT to the weaker CLOSE.

findings.rationale: Both refer to a country code, but the OUR term is specifically for the melt‑and‑pour location of steel, whereas schema.org’s addressCountry is a general country property.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org addressCountry is the country component of a postal address, whereas melt-and-pour country is a manufacturing-provenance attribute of a production process, so they denote different concepts despite both carrying an ISO country code.

findings.rationale: The term specifies the country where steel was melted and poured, which is a more specific instance of product origin covered by schema.org’s countryOfOrigin.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties; melt-and-pour country is a precise, regulation-specific (CBAM) form of product origin that falls under schema.org's deliberately broad, context-varying countryOfOrigin, making ours the narrower term.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the purchaser order reference linked to a steel delivery, sharing identical domain and range.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties denoting a purchaser order reference, but the upstream term has no definition and is generic (not steel-specific), so without a stated domain/semantics for the dppk term, identity cannot be confirmed and the weaker overlapping grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both represent an identifier for a transaction, but the OUR term is specifically a purchaser order reference for steel delivery.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/orderNumber is a generic transaction identifier whose meaning is fully contained within our purchaser-order reference, but ours is scoped specifically to a steel purchaser order, making ours the narrower concept and the upstream term the broader one.

findings.rationale: Both terms share the same name, domain (MaterialTestCertificate), and represent the steelmaking process recorded on a certificate.
findings.qaRationale: Identical property name and MaterialTestCertificate domain strongly suggest the same steelmaking-process concept, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm it shares our free-text scoping rather than a controlled-route semantics, so EXACT interchangeability cannot be asserted.

findings.rationale: Both refer to the steelmaking process recorded on a material test certificate, but the upstream term lacks a definition and may be broader.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term 'mtc' has no definition and its name suggests it denotes a Material Test Certificate (a class/entity), not the steelmaking-process string property our term represents, so it is not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the carbon content measured in a material test certificate, with identical scope and range.
findings.qaRationale: Identical property name and type strongly suggest the same MTC carbon-content concept, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm it shares our cast/ladle-analysis scope and units, so interchangeability cannot be asserted.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same name and refer to the radiometric control result for steel, indicating identical concept.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term shares an identical, highly specific name and property type, but provides no definition to confirm identical semantics, so interchangeability cannot be asserted and the weaker grade is preferred.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same name and refer to the primary steelmaking route used for a product, indicating identical concept.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream property shares the same name and a steel-DPP context, but with no upstream definition I cannot confirm interchangeable semantics, so I downgrade the first pass's EXACT to the weaker CLOSE.

findings.rationale: Both terms represent the same measurement of copper content in a material test certificate.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term has no definition, so identical semantics (cast-analysis copper content in mass %, scoped to a material test certificate) cannot be confirmed; absent that, a same-name property warrants only an approximate match rather than asserted interchangeability.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the manufacturer’s product or article number for a steel product.
findings.qaRationale: Names align and both are properties, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm it denotes the same manufacturer article-number concept, so interchangeable EXACT cannot be justified.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the manufacturer’s part or product number used to identify a specific steel product.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote a manufacturer-assigned product/part identifier string, but schema.org/mpn is framed as a component 'part number' on a broad Product/Offer domain while ours is the article number for a finished steel product, so they overlap closely without being strictly interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The schema.org property represents a general serial or alphanumeric identifier for any product, whereas the OUR term specifically denotes the manufacturer’s product/article number for a steel product.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org serialNumber denotes a per-instance serial/alphanumeric product identifier, while our productNumber is a manufacturer's model/article number; these overlap as product identifiers but our term is the narrower, more specific notion so NARROW is acceptable though CLOSE is defensible.

findings.rationale: The GS1 property denotes a general global model number for any product, whereas the OUR term specifies a manufacturer’s product/article number specifically for steel products.
findings.qaRationale: Both identify a product model/article, but a free-text manufacturer article number is not a kind of GS1-issued Global Model Number identifier, so they overlap rather than one being a strict subtype—NARROW is too strong.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same metric – the relative rib area (fR) for ribbed reinforcing steel used in material test certificates.
findings.qaRationale: The identical highly-specific compound name strongly implies the same fR relative-rib-area metric, but the upstream term carries no definition, domain, or range to confirm true interchangeability, so the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same concept of linking a steel product to its Material Test Certificate.
findings.qaRationale: The matching label and steel-DPP context strongly suggest the same Material Test Certificate concept, but the upstream term has no definition, domain, or range to confirm true interchangeability, so EXACT cannot be asserted.

findings.rationale: Both properties identify the melt or batch number of steel from which a product was cast, matching the same concept.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term shares an identical, highly domain-specific name strongly implying the same steel heat/melt batch concept, but with no upstream definition, domain, or range I cannot confirm true interchangeability, so the weaker CLOSE grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: Heat Number is a specific type of batch/lot identifier for molten steel, making it narrower than the generic Batch/Lot Identifier.
findings.qaRationale: Heat number is a steel-specific production-lot identifier and GS1's batch/lot definition explicitly enumerates production lot numbers, so ours is a genuine specialization, though the referent (molten-steel melt vs trade-item lot) is not a perfectly clean subsumption.

findings.rationale: The GS1 batchLot property denotes a general batch or lot identifier, while the OUR heatNumber specifically identifies the melt (heat) number of steel.
findings.qaRationale: A heat number identifies a metallurgical melt batch at casting for traceability, which overlaps with but is not a clean subtype of GS1's commercial trade-item batchLot identifier, so the concepts are related but not strictly hierarchical.

findings.rationale: Both terms are named lotNumber and refer to a delivery or production batch identifier for the steel product.
findings.qaRationale: Both are lot-number string identifiers so the concept aligns, but the upstream term has no definition and is generic while ours is domain-restricted to IronSteelProduct, so interchangeability cannot be confirmed and EXACT is too strong.

findings.rationale: The GS1 property is a generic batch/lot identifier for any trade item, while the OUR term specifies a delivery lot number for steel products.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a steel-specific delivery lot number constrained to IronSteelProduct, a specialization of GS1's general batch/lot identifier for any trade item, so it is narrower.

findings.rationale: The upstream productNumber is a general identifier for any product, whereas the OUR lotNumber specifically denotes the delivery lot number of a steel product.
findings.qaRationale: A delivery lot number identifying a production/shipment batch is not the same concept as a product identifier (productNumber/SKU), and lot-level identity is distinct from product-level identity, so they are different concepts rather than a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: The GS1 batchLot property is a general batch/lot identifier, whereas the OUR lotNumber specifically denotes the delivery lot number of a steel product.
findings.qaRationale: GS1 batchLot is a generic batch/lot identifier property and our lotNumber is a string-valued delivery-lot identifier for steel; they denote the same property concept narrowed by domain rather than a true sub-concept, so closeMatch is the defensible grade.

findings.rationale: The Steel Production Technology Route is a specific type of technology that influences energy consumption, whereas EnergyConsumptionDetails is a general class covering all products’ energy efficiency information.
findings.qaRationale: A steelmaking process route (e.g. BF-BOF vs EAF) is a manufacturing-method concept, not energy-efficiency labeling information for an energy-consuming product, so the two classes are about different things despite both touching on energy.

findings.rationale: The upstream mtc property represents a general Material Test Certificate concept, while the OUR property specifies a particular inspection document type.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream 'mtc' is an undefined, generic Material Test Certificate property whereas ours is specifically the EN 10204 inspection document type code, so they overlap in subject matter but are not in a clean broader/narrower hierarchy.

findings.rationale: The MTC Inspection Document Type is a specific kind of certification document, whereas Certification Type is a general property for any certification.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties typing a certification/inspection document, but EN 10204 inspection type encodes the inspection regime and validation independence (2.x vs 3.x) rather than a certification scheme category, so they overlap without clean subsumption.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same property measuring the ratio of yield strength to tensile strength.
findings.qaRationale: The identical, highly specific name strongly implies the same yield-to-tensile ratio concept, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm it is the same Re/Rm measure, so a weaker grade than EXACT is warranted.

findings.rationale: The upstream property represents yield strength itself, while the OUR property specifies a derived ratio of yield to tensile strength.
findings.qaRationale: A dimensionless yield-to-tensile ratio (Re/Rm) is a distinct derived quantity, not a narrower form of absolute yield strength, so the concepts do not match.

findings.rationale: Both terms refer to the same concept of permitted weight tolerance for a tested product, and they share identical labels and domain.
findings.qaRationale: Identical property name and type strongly suggest the same weight-tolerance concept, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm interchangeability, so EXACT cannot be asserted.

findings.rationale: Both express a percentage of permissible weight variation, but the GS1 term focuses on yield loss while ours refers to tested product tolerance.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a steel material-test-certificate dimensional/weight tolerance spec; the GS1 term is food cooking/roasting yield variation, an unrelated concept despite both being weight percentages.

findings.rationale: Both terms share the same label, domain (MaterialTestCertificate), and refer to the carbon equivalent value used as a weldability indicator.
findings.qaRationale: Identical IRI fragment, label, and property type for a highly specific concept (carbon equivalent value on a material test certificate), making them interchangeable despite the upstream definition being absent.

findings.rationale: Both terms use the same label and refer to the classification of steel grades according to EN 10020 for iron/steel products, indicating identical scope.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties with the same label and likely the same steel-grade concept, but the upstream term has no definition so EN 10020 scope identity cannot be confirmed, warranting the weaker grade.

findings.rationale: The upstream term refers to a general steel designation, while the OUR property specifies a particular classification scheme (EN 10020).
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties describing a steel grade/designation, but ours is scheme-specific (EN 10020 classification) while the undefined upstream steelDesignation likely denotes the specific grade name/identifier rather than its classification category, so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same inspection document for metallic products, matching in class type and intended use.
findings.qaRationale: Identical class name and type strongly suggest the same concept, but the upstream term has no definition while ours is tightly scoped to EN 10204/EN 10168 metallic-product certificates, so EXACT interchangeability cannot be confirmed and the weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: The OUR class is a specific certification for metallic products per EN 10204, while the upstream class represents any generic certification.
findings.qaRationale: Our EN 10204 material test certificate is a specific kind of product certification, so it is narrower than GS1's generic Certification class, which legitimately covers product certifications.

findings.rationale: Material Test Certificate is a specific type of certification for metallic products, whereas Certification in schema.org is a general concept covering all kinds of certifications.
findings.qaRationale: A material test certificate is an EN 10204 inspection/test document recording measured batch values, which overlaps with schema:Certification's attestation-of-characteristics intent but is not cleanly subsumed by it since types 2.1/2.2 are the manufacturer's own declarations rather than an independent certification body's authoritative credential.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the phosphorus content measured in a material test certificate, matching in type and intended value.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term shares the same name and apparent intent (MTC phosphorus content) but has no definition, so its scope, basis (cast vs product analysis), and units cannot be confirmed as identical, precluding an EXACT match.

findings.rationale: Both properties are named mtcElongation and refer to the same mechanical test result (percentage elongation after fracture) for a material test certificate.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term shares the name and likely refers to certificate elongation, but with no definition provided I cannot confirm it means percentage elongation after fracture (A%) versus another elongation measure, so I downgrade from EXACT.

findings.rationale: Both terms denote the same concept of an iron or steel product, and share identical labels.
findings.qaRationale: Both are iron/steel product classes with identical labels, but ours is tightly scoped to the ESPR delegated act as a carrier for specific identification/MTC data while the upstream term is undefined and presumably generic, so identity cannot be confirmed.

findings.rationale: Both classes represent the same concept of an iron or steel product used in EPCIS contexts.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream candidate has no definition and its label "IronSteel" reads as a material/substance concept rather than the product entity our class denotes, so same-concept identity cannot be established and EXACT is unwarranted.

findings.rationale: Both terms are the same property name and refer to an identifier for a CBAM report linked to a steel product.
findings.qaRationale: Both are string-typed properties identifying the CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism) report associated with a steel product, with identical names and matching semantics.

findings.rationale: Both properties identify the same casting run number within a heat, matching in definition and domain.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term shares the exact name and is plausibly the same casting-run identifier, but with no upstream definition to confirm scope (e.g., cast vs. heat number under EN 10168) interchangeability cannot be asserted, so a weaker CLOSE is warranted.

findings.rationale: The OUR property identifies a casting run within a heat, whereas the upstream property refers to the heat itself, making it more general.
findings.qaRationale: Cast number identifies a casting run within a heat, so it is a finer-grained subdivision of the upstream heat number, making OUR term narrower than the heat identifier.

findings.rationale: The cast number is a specific type of batch/lot identifier used for traceability of a casting run, whereas the GS1 property is a generic batch/lot identifier.
findings.qaRationale: A cast number is a steel-specific production-lot identifier that falls within GS1's explicitly broad batch/lot concept (which names 'production lot number' among its examples), so our term is a narrower specialization of the same property.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same name and refer to the nominal dimension of a tested product, indicating identical meaning.
findings.qaRationale: The identical, highly specific name (MTC nominal size) strongly suggests the same concept, but the upstream term has no definition to confirm identical semantics, units, or range, so interchangeability cannot be asserted.

findings.rationale: The GS1 sizeDimension is a general numerical size measurement, whereas the OUR property specifies the nominal dimension of a tested product.
findings.qaRationale: GS1 sizeDimension is a general product packaging/trade-item size measurement, not the nominal test-specimen dimension recorded on a steel material test certificate, so the concepts do not align despite both being numeric sizes.

findings.rationale: Schema.org size is a general property for any product dimension, while the OUR term specifies a numeric nominal size of a tested material.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific numeric nominal dimension (mm) of a steel test product, which is one constrained sub-case of schema.org's general product size property, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: The upstream term represents the overall Material Test Certificate, whereas our property specifies a particular tensile strength measurement.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream term's label is 'mtc' with no definition, most plausibly denoting the Material Test Certificate as a whole rather than a specific tensile-strength measurement, so our specific decimal property is not interchangeable with it; absent any definition I cannot confirm a clean broader/narrower hierarchy, and treat the weak overlap as CLOSE at best.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same concept of a steel name/number designation.
findings.qaRationale: Identical name and property type in a steel-focused spec point to the same concept, but the upstream term lacks any definition so its exact scope (EN 10027 name/number designation) cannot be confirmed as interchangeable.

findings.rationale: The candidate term refers to a general classification of steel grades, whereas the OUR term specifies a particular designation per EN 10027.
findings.qaRationale: Both are steel-identification properties that overlap, but a 'grade classification' (category/family of steel) is not strictly a superset of an EN 10027 name/number designation, and with no upstream definition a clean NARROW subsumption can't be asserted.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same name and refer to the measured yield strength of a material test certificate.
findings.qaRationale: Names and MTC-property context align closely, but the upstream term carries no definition to confirm identical semantics (measurement Re, MPa, decimal), so interchangeability cannot be asserted and a weaker grade is warranted.

findings.rationale: The upstream property represents a generic MTC concept, whereas our term specifies the measured yield strength attribute of an MTC.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a specific decimal-valued measurement attribute (yield strength Re) of a certificate, while the upstream 'mtc' is an undefined generic property representing the certificate concept itself; differing referents and no definition make them not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties share the same name and refer to nitrogen content measured in a material test certificate.
findings.qaRationale: The identical mtc-prefixed name and property type make it the same nitrogen-content concept, but with no upstream definition I cannot confirm it specifies cast (heat) analysis rather than product analysis, so I withhold EXACT.

findings.rationale: The candidate property describes a generic material composition percentage, whereas our term specifies the nitrogen content of a steel MTC.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a steel cast-analysis nitrogen mass-percentage on a MaterialTestCertificate while the GS1 term is a textile material composition description; different domains and concepts entirely, not a broader/narrower relation.
